label: Visions of Amram (4QAmram) — Complete
summary_plain: The Visions of Amram record the deathbed vision of Amram — father of Moshe, Aharon, and Miryam. Amram sees two beings contending over him — one of light and one of darkness. The being of light is Malki-Tzedek (King of Righteousness). The being of darkness is Malki-Resha (King of Wickedness). Amram must choose between them. The vision establishes the cosmic dualism that runs through the entire Qumran literature — Sons of Light versus Sons of Darkness.
summary_scholarly: 4QAmram (4Q543-548) is preserved in Aramaic fragments from Qumran Cave 4. The text is a testament — Amram's words to his sons before death. The cosmic dualism between Malki-Tzedek and Malki-Resha parallels the War Scroll's Sons of Light versus Sons of Darkness framework. The text establishes that the covenant struggle between light and darkness precedes the Mosaic covenant — it is already operative in the generation before Sinai.

key_terms.definition: Father of Moshe, Aharon, and Miryam. A Levite. His testament records a vision of two cosmic beings — one of light, one of darkness — contending over his destiny.
King of Righteousness — the being of light in Amram's vision. The same figure appears in Bereshit 14 (the priest-king of Shalem who blesses Avram) and in 11QMelchizedek at Qumran.
King of Wickedness — the being of darkness. The adversarial counterpart to Malki-Tzedek. This dualism structures the War Scroll framework.

fill_blank.prompt: Amram saw two beings contending over him — one of ______ and one of darkness.
fill_blank.source_quote: Amram saw two beings — one of light and one of darkness — contending over his destiny.

fill_blank.prompt: The being of light was called Malki-Tzedek, which means King of ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: The being of light is Malki-Tzedek — King of Righteousness.

multiple_choice.question: Who is Amram in the covenant lineage?
options: Father of Moshe, Aharon, and Miryam
multiple_choice.source_quote: Amram — father of Moshe, Aharon, and Miryam.

multiple_choice.question: What does Malki-Resha mean?
multiple_choice.source_quote: Malki-Resha — King of Wickedness — the being of darkness.

faq.question: How does the Amram vision connect to the War Scroll?
faq.answer: The cosmic dualism in Amram's vision — Malki-Tzedek (light) versus Malki-Resha (darkness) — is the same framework that structures the entire War Scroll (1QM): Sons of Light versus Sons of Darkness. The Amram text establishes that this cosmic struggle precedes the Mosaic covenant — it is already operative in the generation before Sinai. The covenant people's struggle against domination is not a historical accident. It is part of a cosmic contest between righteousness and wickedness that the ancient texts describe as bounded and destined for resolution.

faq.question: What does 11QMelchizedek add to the Amram vision?
faq.answer: 11QMelchizedek — a Qumran Cave 11 text — expands the Malki-Tzedek figure into a cosmic agent of YHWH's final judgment. Malki-Tzedek proclaims liberty (the Jubilee) and executes judgment against Belial (the adversary). The Amram vision shows Malki-Tzedek contending over an individual. 11QMelchizedek shows him executing judgment at the cosmic level. The Jubilee framework from Vayikra 25 — 'proclaim liberty throughout the land' — is applied by Malki-Tzedek at the end of the age. This is not Christian christology — Malki-Tzedek in the DSS is an angelic figure executing YHWH's justice, not a divine incarnation.
